extracted.brandBackground: Anytime Fitness offers 24-hour fitness centers with convenient access and coaching services.

leadership.name: Thomas Leverton
leadership.background: Served as CEO of parent companies since November 2024 and was previously a partner at Pritzker Private Capital.
leadership.whyItMatters: Provides senior private equity and corporate leadership experience to the brand.

leadership.name: Charles Runyon
leadership.background: Co-founder of Anytime Fitness and served as CEO from October 2021 to November 2024.
leadership.whyItMatters: Maintains the original brand vision and long-term continuity.

leadership.name: Dave Mortensen
leadership.background: Co-founder of Anytime Fitness and served as President from October 2021 to November 2024.
leadership.whyItMatters: Ensures foundational operational knowledge remains on the board.

leadership.name: Stacy Anderson
leadership.background: Served as Global Brand President since January 2024 and Brand President since August 2016.
leadership.whyItMatters: Directs global brand strategy and marketing consistency.

leadership.name: Robert Gunkel
leadership.background: Interim CFO since January 2026 and previously served as EVP and Treasurer for Sonesta RL Hotels.
leadership.whyItMatters: Oversees financial planning and franchise system economics.

leadership.name: James Goniea
leadership.background: General Counsel since October 2021 and previously held GC roles with predecessor AFLLC since 2017.
leadership.whyItMatters: Manages legal compliance, franchise agreements, and regulatory filings.

operationalRisks.title: Out-of-State Dispute Resolution
operationalRisks.description: Disputes must be resolved by mediation, arbitration, or litigation in Minnesota.

operationalRisks.title: Spousal Liability
operationalRisks.description: Spouses must sign a personal guarantee making them liable for all financial obligations.

operationalRisks.title: Supplier Control
operationalRisks.description: Franchisees must purchase nearly all supplies and technology from designated vendors.

extracted.conceptRationale: The brand operates 24-hour fitness centers offering gym access and coaching services.
extracted.staffingRationale: The concept is designed to allow members 24-hour keyless access, operating unstaffed during off-peak hours.

riskReasons: Rent is heavy: ~31% of mid-cohort revenue (above 25%).
A high-severity operational tripwire is disclosed — see the tripwires section.
Franchisor financial distress disclosed (negative net worth) — confirm its ability to support the system against the audited statements.

midCohort.label: All Franchised Centers Operating With AF Coaching - Membership Revenue

source.label: All Franchised Centers Operating With AF Coaching - Membership Revenue

underwriting.assessment: With $250,000 liquid against a $722,406 mid-point build-out, there is a $472,406 capital gap. An SBA loan or an equity partner is required to do this deal. At mid-cohort revenue, modeled cash flow after debt service is about $10,767/mo before payroll and maintenance — a thin margin of error.

insights.conceptRationale: The brand operates 24-hour fitness centers offering gym access and coaching services.

benchmark.label: Boutique fitness / studio
benchmark.dominantRisk: Member retention/churn and fixed-cost coverage — NOT cost of goods. The deal is decided by recurring membership against rent and equipment lease.
benchmark.rampNote: 9–18 months to a mature, retained member base.
considerations: COGS is trivial; the risks are member churn and covering fixed rent + equipment lease.
Ask Item 20 operators for monthly retention, average membership lifetime, and break-even member count.
Model CAC and free-trial conversion — member acquisition cost is the hidden operating line.

crossCheck.message: No operating-EBITDA margin is disclosed in Item 19. The 15–28% band below is an industry estimate for this concept — validate it against real franchisee P&Ls (Item 20) before relying on any margin.

insights.staffingNote: Flagged as an automated / minimal-staff model from the FDD, so labor is adjusted down to 5–12% — the 20–30% category band assumes a staffed venue. Confirm actual staffing with Item 20 franchisees.

buildup.label: − Labor
buildup.note: automated / minimal-staff · ≈ 0.4–1 FTE at ~$20/hr fully loaded

assumptions.detail: Classified as “Boutique fitness / studio” from the FDD (Item 1 / brand description).
Read as automated / minimal-staff from the FDD, which lowers the labor band applied below.
Taken from the FDD (~$8,745/mo); already netted in margin after fees & rent.
Estimated at the 3–10% category band — COGS is never disclosed in an FDD.
Estimated at the 5–12% automated / minimal-staff band — labor is never disclosed in an FDD.
Utilities, insurance, and R&M estimated at 6–10% (category catch-all).
Our calculation — the disclosed top line minus the benchmarked costs above. Not a figure stated in the FDD.

insights.consultCtaPitch: These are category benchmarks applied to the disclosed top line — your unit's real costs will differ, and that difference is the conversation. Want them pressure-tested against actual franchisee P&Ls for your territory?
insights.asOf: 2026 — general industry benchmarks, refine against real franchisee P&Ls
insights.disclaimer: Industry benchmark ranges for your own budgeting and Item 20 questions — NOT a projection of this franchise's results, and not investment advice.

financialCondition.headline: This franchisor has negative net worth (about $932.05M in the red) — it owes more than it owns — though the trend isn't worsening. Understand what's funding the gap before you commit.
financialCondition.context: Worth perspective: the auditor issued a clean (unmodified) opinion and did not raise going-concern doubt; revenue is growing; the business is profitable; the parent guarantees the franchisor's performance. Early-stage franchisors commonly run losses and carry a deficit while investing to scale — this reads more like spending ahead of revenue than a failing business. The real question is runway: how the gap is funded, and for how long.
body: What we found (2025, audited by PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP): Net income of $154.91M on $375.93M of revenue — roughly 0.6x spent per $1 earned. Negative net worth (a members'/stockholders' deficit) of $932.05M. Total liabilities are about 9.7x total assets. Cash of $19.32M against $38.56M of obligations due within a year (a current ratio of 1.82).

Why it matters to you: field support, training, technology, and marketing come out of the franchisor's budget. If outside funding slows, those are the first things to get cut. Purpose Brands Holdings, LLC guarantees the franchisor's performance, which partly backstops this.

Verify before you commit: ask for the most recent interim (unaudited) financials and the current-year funding plan; ask whether the parent will sign a guarantee of performance; and cross-check Item 20 — unit closures, transfers, and terminations are the operational tell that corroborates financial stress.
aggravators: negative net worth — it owes more than it owns
mitigants: the auditor issued a clean (unmodified) opinion and did not raise going-concern doubt
the parent guarantees the franchisor's performance
financialCondition.evidenceNote: Flagged on the FDD's "Special Risks" page and grounded in the Item 21 / Exhibit F audited financials. Figures are extracted; all ratios and grading are computed. Confirm against the source statements and a CPA before relying on them.
